content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 22, 2012
Tej Kohli
7749 Elmsmere Ct
Elk Grove, CA 96758

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our file No. I-12-004

Dear Mr. Kohli:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post-governmental employment laws such as Public Contract Code Section 10411.

QUESTION

May you work part-time as a consultant for companies that have been bidders on projects that you have worked on as a state employee while you are still a state employee and may you work for these companies full-time after you have separated from State service?

FACTS

You are currently an employee of the Department of Finance working for the FiSCal project. You are planning to retire sometime this year. You are requesting legal advice concerning your future employment opportunities in the private sector during your final year of employment and after your retirement from State service.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114, Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. I-12-004
Page No. 2

Currently you serve as a FiSCal Project Enterprise Architect. This is a designated
position in your agency's conflict-of-interest code. This project you have been working on is a
large-scale IT project. The project is still in the procurement phase. The winning bidder is
expected to be selected and announced on February 15th or later. Your role has been that of an
Enterprise Architect on this project with basic responsibilities related to technical architecture in
the RFP and the procurement process. Your duties have included refining technical
requirements, formulating technical plans and deliverables and defining target architecture
required for the project. There is a separate evaluation team that takes the specifications that you
have helped develop and decides which bidders should win the project contract.

Prior to joining the FiSCal project you were with the Franchise Tax Board working as a
Technical Manager on the EDR project from February 2007 through May 2010. At the time you
left that project was in the procurement phase, the bidder had not been selected, and the contract
award had not been made. Your role in that project was related to defining and refining all
technical requirements, technical plans and deliverables, target architecture and other aspects
technical in nature. The EDR contract was awarded to CGI in July 2011. Again, while you were
involved in developing the specifications for the project you were not involved in the selection of
the winning bidder for this project; the selection for the winning bidder was done by an
independent evaluation team.

A large part of your career before State service had been in the private sector with various
companies. You would like to work part-time as an independent consultant during the final year
of your employment with the State and then work as a full-time consultant after retirement.

Specifically, you would like to know if you can work-part time as a consultant for
companies that are bidders of the FiSCal project or for companies that are not involved with the
FiSCal project while you still work for the State, and if you can work full or part-time as a
consultant for the FiSCal project either directly or through the winning bidder for the project or
its subcontractors? Additionally, you would like to know if after the date of your retirement you
may work as a part-time or full-time consultant for EDR or other State projects?

ANALYSIS AND CONCLUSIONS

The Act does not prohibit you from accepting a position with any of the consulting
companies you have mentioned. However, you should check with your agency and determine if
acceptance of a part-time position while still working for your agency would violate your
agency's incompatible activities policies. Your inquiry does, however, raise questions under the
Act's negotiation of prospective employment and post-governmental employment provisions.

Negotiating Prospective Employment

All public officials, leaving governmental service are subject to restrictions when
negotiating prospective employment. Under Section 87407 and Regulation 18747, prior to
separation from government service, a public official is prohibited from making, participating in

File No. I-12-004
Page No. 3

making, or influencing a "governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom he or
she is negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, prospective employment." In addition,
the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit an official from making, participating in
making, or influencing any governmental decision with a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the source of promised income. (See Sections 87100, et seq. and Regulations
18700, et seq.)

Section 87407 prohibits certain state and local officials from making, participating in
making, or using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they
are negotiating employment, or have any arrangement concerning employment. While a public
official may negotiate and accept an offer of future employment before leaving his or her current
position, Section 87407 is designed to ensure that the official does not use his or her state
position to make any decisions that unduly benefit the organization that is hiring the official.

Section 87407 states:

"No public official shall make, participate in making, or use his or her official
position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any
person with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement
concerning, prospective employment."

The term "public official" is defined, in part, in Section 82048 as "every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . . ." The Department of Finance
is a state agency, and as an employee of that agency you are subject to Section 87407.

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purposes of
influencing, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant
of his or her agency (or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the
official's agency) or acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer, employee, or consultant of any other agency. (Regulation
18702.3.)

A public official is considered to be "negotiating employment" when he or she interviews
or discusses an offer of employment with an employer or his or her agent. (Regulation
18747(c)(1).) The Commission has construed the scheduling, conduct, and follow-up to an
interview as one continuous process falling under the definition of "negotiating" employment.
(Brunner Advice Letter, No. 1-98-287.) However, the mere act of sending a resume or application

File No. I-12-004
Page No. 6


Generally, Regulation 18746.2 provides that an appearance or communication is for the purpose
of influencing if "made for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing,
modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing" an action or proceeding.

You may make "casual contacts" with employees of your former agencies that are
assigned to work the systems for which you helped create the specifications. However, in your
work in the private sector you may not make any recommendations to your former agencies or
their employees regarding the projects. So long as your contacts with you former agencies are
not for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing,
delaying, or advancing an action or proceeding in which you participated, your contacts with
your former agencies as a contractor would not be prohibited under the permanent ban.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

May you work-part time as a consultant for companies that have been bidders on projects that 
you have worked on as a state employee while you are still a state employee and may you work 
for these companies full-time after you have separated from State service?

sections.analysis:
AND CONCLUSIONS 
The Act does not prohibit you from accepting a position with any of the consulting 
companies you have mentioned. However, you should check with your agency and determine if 
acceptance of a part-time position while still working for your agency would violate your 
agency's incompatible activities policies. Your inquiry does, however, raise questions under the 
Act's negotiation of prospective employment and post-governmental employment.provisions. 
Negotiating Prospective Employment 
All public officials. leaving governmental service are :mbject to restrictions when 
negotiating prospective employment. Under Section 87407 and Regulation 18747, prior to 
separation from government service. a public official is prohibited from making, participating in  
making, or influencing a "governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom he or 
she is negotiating, or ha-; any arrangement concerning, prospective employment.'' In addition, 
the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit an official from making, participating in 
making, or influencing any governmental decision with a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on the source of promised income. (See Sections 87100. et seq. and Regulations 
18700. et seq.) 
Section 87407 prohibits certain slate and local officials from making, pmticipating in 
making, or using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they 
arc negotiating employment, or have any arrangement concerning employment. While a public 
official may negotiate and accept _an offer of future employment before leaving his or her current 
state position, Section 87407 is designed to ensure that the official does not use his or her state 
position to make any decisions that unduly benefit the organization that is biting the official. 
Section 87407 states: 
"No public official shall make, participate in making. or use his or her official 
position to influence, any govemmcntal decision directly relating to any 
person with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any arrangement 
concerning, prospective employment.·• 
The term "public official" is defined, in part. in Section 82048 as "every member. officer, 
employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . ... " The Department of Finance 
is a state agency. and as an employee of that agency you are subject to Section 87407. 
A public official .. makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the 
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her 
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her 
agency. (Regulation 18702. L) A public official "paiticipates in a governmental decision" when. 
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive 
review. the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker 
regarding the governmental qecision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to 
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if. for the purposes of 
influencing. the official contacts or appears before any member. officer, employee, or consult.mt 
of his or her agency (or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the 
official· s agency) or acts or purports to act on behalf of. or as the representative of, his or her 
agency to any member, officer, employee. or consultant of any other agency. (Regulation 
18702.3.) 
!, 
A public of
ficial is considered lo be "negotiating employment·· ,vhen he or she interviews 
or discusses an offer of employment with an employer or his or her agent. (Regulation 
18747(c)(l).) The Commission has constmcd the scheduling. conduct. and follow-up to an 
interview as one continuous process falling under Lhe definition of "negotiating" employment. 
(B01111£•r Ad1•ice Letter. No. 1-98-287.) However, the mere act of sending a resume or application 
..  
to a specific entity has not been considered "negotiating:· Similarly. entertaining infonnal 
inquiries about future plans and receiving expressions of general interest in discussing potential 
employment opportunities at some point in the future are not considered "negotiating.'' (Id.) .. A 
public official has an 'arrangement' concerning prospective employment when he or she accepts 
an employer's offer of employment." (Regulation 18747(c)(2).) 
Once you have "negotiated" or have an "arrangement" with a prospective employer as 
discussed above, you are prohibited under Section 87407 from making, participating in making, 
or using yow- official position to influence any governmental decisions "directly relating" to the 
employer. Regulation 18747(b) provides that a governmental decision "directly relates·• to a 
prospective employer if the public official knows or has reason to know the employer is "directly 
involved'' in the decision, as defined in Regulation 18704.l(a), or that it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the financial effect of a decision on a prospective employer is material. 
Under Regulation 18704. l(a), a prospective employer is "directly involved" in a 
governmental decision when that employer either directly or by agent (1) initiates the proceeding 
in which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or 
(2) is a named paity in-, or subject of, the proceeding. A prospective employer is the ·•subject of" 
.the proceeding if a decision involves the issuance. renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any 
license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract, with the prospective employer. 
The Post Governmental Employment Provisions 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the 
0one-year ban" and the "pennanent ban," colloquially 
known as the "revolving door" prohibitions. 3 
The One-Year Ban 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state agency who holds a position that is 
designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Section 87406(d) 
(1); Regulation 18746.1 (a) (2).) Because the position you now hold is designated in your 
agency's conflict-of-interest code, the one-year ban will apply to you once you leave state 
service. Therefore, you would be prohibited for one year from conversing by telephone or in 
person, corresponding in writing or by electronic communication, attending a meeting, and 
delivering or sending any communication to the Department of Finance if any of these actions 
are done for the purpose of influencing governmental decisions made by your former agency. 
(Regulation 18746.2.) The one-year ban does not apply to formal participation in a panel or 
conference for educational purposes or to di§seminate research to your former agency, 
attendance at general informational meetings of your former agency, seminars, or similar events, 
3 In addition, Section 87407 prohibits certain state and local officials from making, participating in making, 
or using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating employment, 
or have any arrangement concerning employment. (Also see Regulation 18747.) 
.  
making requests for information about any matter of public record from your former agency or 
communications with the press.4 
The enclosed fact sheet "Leaving Your State Job: Revolving Door and Other Post4 
Employment Issues for State Officials" describes in further detail the activities that are 
prohibited by this ban. 
The Permanent Ban 
The permanent ban applies to every "state administrative official." which is defined as 
"every member. officer, employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of 
his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in 
other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity." (Section 87400(b).) The 
permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in 
which a state administrative official participated while he or she served as a state administrative 
official. "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" means any proceeding, application. request 
for a ruling or other determination. contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, 
accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or 
state administrative agency . . .. " (Section 87400(c).) 
You have had two positions with the State, one at the Franchise Tax Board and your 
current position at the Department of Finance. You have performed similar work at both 
positions. 
As an EDR Project Technical Manager from February 2007 through May 2010 at the 
Franchise Tax Board, you stated that you worked on refining technical requirements, technical 
plans and deliverables, etc. There was a separate evaluation team that examined the technical 
requirements you helped create and determined which bidder was awarded the project. 
With regard to your position with the Department of Finance as a Fi$Cal Project 
Enterprise Architect you have been involved in the technical architecture in the RFP and the 
procurement process, this includes refining technical requirements, fonnulating technical plɳs 
and deliverables and defining target architecture required for the project. The winning bidder 
has not yet been selected but is expected to be selected on February 15th or later. 
Under, the Act the contract between the both the FTB nnd Fi$Cal and the winning 
bidders for the projects you helped with are considered "proceedings·• involving a specific party, 
and you have '"participated'' in proceedings by rendering a decision as to what specifications 
t. were required for the project. Accordingly, the Act's permanent ban \\ouh.1 prohibit you from 
":-.witching sides .. making any appearance before or conununication with any officer or employer 
of any state administrative agency for the purpose of influencing, as defined in Regulation 
18746.2, the proceeding in which you previously paiticipatcd. (Regulation 18741.l(a)(3).) 
4 The one year ban no longer applies to your position at the Franchise Tax Board because it has been over 
one year since you left that position. 
..  
Generally. Regulation 18746.2 provides that an appearance or communication is for the purpose 
of influencing if "made for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting. influencing. 
modifying, opposing, delaying. or advancing" an action or proceeding. 
You may make .. casual contacts" with employees of your fonner agencies tl1at are 
assigned to work the systems for which you helped create the specifications. However, in your 
work in the private sector you may not make any recommendations to your former agencies or 
their employees regarding the projects. So long as your contacts with your fom1er agencies are 
not for the principal purpose of supporting. promoting, influencing, modifying. opposing, 
delaying. or advancing an action or proceeding in which you participated, your contacts with 
your former agencies as a contractor would not be prohibited under the pem1:1nent ban.
